The forest-sentinels, they said, grew tired of chaos and began to appoint moralizing agents: guardians who sounded like fathers, judges who wore the night as a veil. At first these agents spoke softly: do not steal, do not lie, keep the hunter's covenant. But as communities coalesced and farms hatched from sun-baked soils, the agents grew more potent, their words braided with rain and fire, until a whole sky spoke through edicts etched in stone, clay, and song.

To prove their faith credible, people staged displays—rigid ceremonies, conspicuous fasting, processions that moved like a driven river. The rituals stirred inner drums, made breath synchronize, and turned fear into shared rhythm, a psychology of togetherness that bound rival bands into one fragile loom.

High birthrates followed, as fertile soil and fearless rites fed masses who carried stories outward. In every valley, intergroup conflict sharpened into competition, and to copy was to conquer: rites learned, myths borrowed, and rules copied with fresh wax across borders. Beliefs emerged as by-products of practice, later tempered by centuries of reflection, memory, and time.
